name: Sharks
description: Shark species, anatomy, behavior, and conservation

questions.q: What is the largest species of fish alive today?

source.notes: Can exceed 18 meters in length; blue whales are mammals, not fish.

questions.q: What is the primary material of a shark's skeleton?

source.notes: Sharks belong to the class Chondrichthyes along with rays and chimaeras.

questions.q: Which shark is the primary antagonist of the 1975 film Jaws?

source.notes: The 'rogue shark' terrorizing Amity Island is a great white.

questions.q: Whale sharks primarily feed on what?

source.notes: Along with basking sharks, they are the main plankton-filtering sharks.

questions.q: Which extinct prehistoric shark is famous for its enormous fossilized teeth?

source.notes: Lived roughly 23-3.6 million years ago; teeth up to 18 cm.

questions.q: How many gill slits do most shark species have on each side?

source.notes: Most species have five; a few primitive species like the cow sharks have six or seven.

questions.q: Which distinctive feature gives hammerhead sharks their name?

source.notes: The flattened, laterally-extended head is called a 'cephalofoil'.

questions.q: In what scientific class are sharks grouped?

source.notes: The cartilaginous fishes.

questions.q: Which shark species is widely cited as the fastest in the ocean, reaching bursts of about 50 km/h?

source.notes: Warm-bodied predator found in temperate and tropical seas.

questions.q: Sharks lack which organ that most bony fish use for buoyancy?

source.notes: They rely on a large oil-filled liver and constant motion for lift.

questions.q: Which electroreceptor organs help sharks detect the faint electrical fields of prey?

source.notes: Appear as tiny pores on the snout; can detect microvolts.

questions.q: Which species is widely considered the longest-lived vertebrate, with individuals estimated to live over 250-400 years?

source.notes: 2016 radiocarbon study estimated ages up to 392 years.

questions.q: What name is given to the tiny tooth-like scales that cover a shark's skin?

source.notes: Also called dermal denticles; improve hydrodynamics.

questions.q: Which shark uses its extremely elongated upper tail lobe to stun prey?

source.notes: Tail can be as long as the body itself.

questions.q: Which species is widely known as the only omnivorous shark on record, eating seagrass alongside prey?

source.notes: A small hammerhead that can digest seagrass effectively.

questions.q: Which shark species is notable for tolerating freshwater and has been found far upstream in rivers?

source.notes: Recorded thousands of km up the Amazon and Mississippi rivers.

questions.q: Which cruel practice, in which fins are removed and the live shark is discarded, is the main driver of shark population declines?

source.notes: Fins are used in shark fin soup, a luxury dish in some East Asian cuisines.

questions.q: Approximately how many species of sharks are currently recognized worldwide?

source.notes: Ranging from tiny dwarf lantern sharks to massive whale sharks.

questions.q: What palm-sized creature is widely considered the smallest known shark species?

source.notes: Etmopterus perryi; adults reach about 20 cm.

questions.q: The great white and mako sharks belong to which order of sharks?

source.notes: Also includes thresher, basking, and goblin sharks.

questions.q: What is the name of the reflective tissue behind a shark's retina that enhances low-light vision?

source.notes: Also responsible for the bright eye-shine in cats and other predators.

questions.q: Which small shark is famous for biting circular plug-shaped chunks out of much larger marine animals?

source.notes: Attaches to whales, dolphins, and even submarines.
